About CogitoCommunity Our Philosophy Active Creation > Passive Consumption
CogitoCommunity is a bilingual student publication that rejects traditional “memorize and regurgitate” education. We believe in:
Applied Knowledge: Learning through creation, not consumption Student-Led Research: Ownership of intellectual pursuits Cross-Pollination: Biology meets Philosophy meets Public Speaking Critical Thinking: Question assumptions, defend positions, build arguments Our Mission To create a platform where students engage in meaningful intellectual discourse, produce original research, and develop skills that matter for college admissions, career readiness, and character development.
What We Cover Biology Club Anatomy, Cognitive Science, Pharmacology, Epidemiology — exploring how life works at every level.
Philosophy Club Logic, Ethics, Epistemology — examining fundamental questions and developing critical thinking frameworks.
Public Speaking Club Rhetoric, Debate, Persuasion — mastering the art of effective communication and argumentation.
The Team Co-Founders: Hussein Yassine & Maria Ghtaimi Core Contributors: to be announced
Content Policy We cover global and theoretical topics across all subjects. We do not publish content on local Lebanese politics to maintain focus on intellectual exploration rather than divisive local issues.

content:
WRITER INSTRUCTIONS
REQUIRED FIELDS
METADATA Title: [Engaging and specific—avoid vague titles like “The Importance of Sleep”]
Subtitle (Optional): [Clarify scope if needed]
Author: [Your Name]
Club Affiliation: [Biology / Philosophy / Public Speaking/ Politics (non-Lebanese)] Date Submitted: [MM/DD/YYYY] Word Count: [Auto-calculate or estimate] Category Tags: [e.g., Neuroscience, Ethics, Rhetoric]
SUMMARY BOX (Required – might skip for short articles) 75-100 words \ Write a standalone summary that answers: What is this article about? What will the reader learn? Why does it matter? Example: “This article explores how dopamine regulation affects student motivation and academic performance. By understanding the neuroscience of reward systems, students can design better study habits and avoid burnout. Practical takeaways include actionable strategies for managing screen time and optimizing focus.”
OPENING HOOK 100-150 words Start with one of the following: A provocative question (“Why do we procrastinate even when we know it hurts us?”) A surprising statistic (“70% of high school students report chronic sleep deprivation”) A vivid anecdote or scenario \ A challenge to conventional wisdom Purpose: Make the reader care. Establish why this topic matters to students right now. \
THESIS STATEMENT 1-2 sentences State your core argument or insight. It should be: Specific (not “Sleep is important” but “Polyphasic sleep schedules may harm long-term memory consolidation”) Debatable or Non-Obvious (if everyone already agrees, it’s not worth writing) \
BODY SECTIONS 300-1200 words (divided into 2-4 subsections) Use descriptive headers (e.g., “The Neuroscience of Habit Formation” instead of “Body Paragraph 1”). Each subsection should: Present Evidence: Cite research, data, expert opinions, or real-world examples Analyze, Don’t Summarize: Explain why this evidence matters and how it supports your thesis Connect to Your Argument: Every paragraph should ladder back to your main point Formatting Tips: Use bullet points or numbered lists sparingly (only when listing is clearer than prose) Include hyperlinks to sources within the text when referencing them Break up long paragraphs (aim for 3-5 sentences per paragraph) \
CRITICAL ANALYSIS (Optional for 500-700 words, Required for 1000+) 100-200 words This is NOT just “the other side of the argument.” Instead, consider: Limitations: What doesn’t this research account for? Counterarguments: What would a skeptic say? Nuance: Under what conditions might your thesis not hold true? Example: “While dopamine-driven gamification can boost short-term motivation, critics argue it may undermine intrinsic interest in learning. Students might become dependent on external rewards, reducing long-term intellectual curiosity. This limitation is worth considering when designing study systems.”
REAL-WORLD APPLICATION 100-150 words Answer: “So what? How can students actually USE this?” Provide: Concrete action steps Mindset shifts Tools or resources Questions for self-reflection Example: “To apply this knowledge: (1) Track your dopamine triggers using a simple journal—what activities leave you energized vs. drained? (2) Replace passive scrolling with ‘active consumption’ (e.g., annotating articles instead of skimming). (3) Experiment with 25-minute focus blocks (Pomodoro) to optimize reward cycles.”
CONCLUSION 75-100 words Restate your thesis in new words (don’t copy-paste) End with a memorable closing line: A thought-provoking question A call to action A bold prediction or statement Avoid: Generic endings like “In conclusion, sleep is important for students.”
CITATIONS (Minimum 2 Required) Format: Use APA 7 style Credibility Standards: ✓ Academic journals (PubMed, JSTOR, Google Scholar) ✓ Reputable news outlets (NYT, BBC, Scientific American) ✓ Primary sources (research papers, official reports, expert interviews) ✗ Wikipedia, personal blogs, unverified websites Citation Examples: Journal: Smith, J. (2023). Dopamine and motivation in adolescents. Journal of Neuroscience, 45(3), 120-135. News: Brown, A. (2024, January 15). The science of procrastination. The New York Times. https://nytimes.com/article Book: Clear, J. (2018). Atomic Habits. Penguin Random House. Include hyperlinks for all online sources.
FLEXIBILITY NOTE This template is a guide, not a straitjacket. If your article requires a different structure (e.g., an interview-based piece, a photo essay with commentary, a comparative analysis), that’s fine—but explain your reasoning in your submission notes.
SUBMISSION CHECKLIST (Before You Submit) Title is specific and engaging Thesis is clear and debatable At least 2 credible sources cited Word count is 500-1500 words Spellcheck and grammar check completed All hyperlinks work Real-world application section answers “So what?” Next Steps: Submit via the CogitoCommunity Google Form. You’ll receive feedback within 5-7 days. Revisions are normal — great writing is rewriting.
Goodluck.

Welcome to CogitoCommunity - a bilingual student publication that prioritizes critique-based thinking over memorization.

Our Mission

We believe in:

  • Applied Knowledge: Learning through creation, not consumption
  • Student-Led Research: Ownership of intellectual pursuits
  • Cross-Pollination: Biology meets Philosophy meets Public Speaking

What We Cover

  • Biology: Anatomy, Cognitive Science, Pharmacology
  • Philosophy: Logic, theory defense, ethical frameworks
  • Public Speaking: Anxiety management, debate, improvisation
  • And much more…

Join Us

This is just the beginning. Stay tuned for:
title: Welcome to CogitoCommunity

content:
About CogitoCommunity Our Philosophy Active Creation > Passive Consumption
CogitoCommunity is a bilingual student publication that rejects traditional “memorize and regurgitate” education. We believe in:
Applied Knowledge: Learning through creation, not consumption Student-Led Research: Ownership of intellectual pursuits Cross-Pollination: Biology meets Philosophy meets Public Speaking Critical Thinking: Question assumptions, defend positions, build arguments Our Mission To create a platform where students engage in meaningful intellectual discourse, produce original research, and develop skills that matter for college admissions, career readiness, and character development.
What We Cover Biology Club Anatomy, Cognitive Science, Pharmacology, Epidemiology — exploring how life works at every level.
Philosophy Club Logic, Ethics, Epistemology — examining fundamental questions and developing critical thinking frameworks.
Public Speaking Club Rhetoric, Debate, Persuasion — mastering the art of effective communication and argumentation.
The Team Co-Founders: Hussein Yassine & Maria Ghtaimi Core Contributors: to be announced
Content Policy We cover global and theoretical topics across all subjects. We do not publish content on local Lebanese politics to maintain focus on intellectual exploration rather than divisive local issues.
Contact Email: communitycogito@gmail.com (might change)

title: About CogitoCommunity

content:
WRITER INSTRUCTIONS
REQUIRED FIELDS
METADATA Title: [Engaging and specific—avoid vague titles like “The Importance of Sleep”]
Subtitle (Optional): [Clarify scope if needed]
Author: [Your Name]
Club Affiliation: [Biology / Philosophy / Public Speaking/ Politics (non-Lebanese)] Date Submitted: [MM/DD/YYYY] Word Count: [Auto-calculate or estimate] Category Tags: [e.g., Neuroscience, Ethics, Rhetoric]
SUMMARY BOX (Required – might skip for short articles) 75-100 words \ Write a standalone summary that answers: What is this article about? What will the reader learn? Why does it matter? Example: “This article explores how dopamine regulation affects student motivation and academic performance. By understanding the neuroscience of reward systems, students can design better study habits and avoid burnout. Practical takeaways include actionable strategies for managing screen time and optimizing focus.”
OPENING HOOK 100-150 words Start with one of the following: A provocative question (“Why do we procrastinate even when we know it hurts us?”) A surprising statistic (“70% of high school students report chronic sleep deprivation”) A vivid anecdote or scenario \ A challenge to conventional wisdom Purpose: Make the reader care. Establish why this topic matters to students right now. \
THESIS STATEMENT 1-2 sentences State your core argument or insight. It should be: Specific (not “Sleep is important” but “Polyphasic sleep schedules may harm long-term memory consolidation”) Debatable or Non-Obvious (if everyone already agrees, it’s not worth writing) \
BODY SECTIONS 300-1200 words (divided into 2-4 subsections) Use descriptive headers (e.g., “The Neuroscience of Habit Formation” instead of “Body Paragraph 1”). Each subsection should: Present Evidence: Cite research, data, expert opinions, or real-world examples Analyze, Don’t Summarize: Explain why this evidence matters and how it supports your thesis Connect to Your Argument: Every paragraph should ladder back to your main point Formatting Tips: Use bullet points or numbered lists sparingly (only when listing is clearer than prose) Include hyperlinks to sources within the text when referencing them Break up long paragraphs (aim for 3-5 sentences per paragraph) \
CRITICAL ANALYSIS (Optional for 500-700 words, Required for 1000+) 100-200 words This is NOT just “the other side of the argument.” Instead, consider: Limitations: What doesn’t this research account for? Counterarguments: What would a skeptic say? Nuance: Under what conditions might your thesis not hold true? Example: “While dopamine-driven gamification can boost short-term motivation, critics argue it may undermine intrinsic interest in learning. Students might become dependent on external rewards, reducing long-term intellectual curiosity. This limitation is worth considering when designing study systems.”
REAL-WORLD APPLICATION 100-150 words Answer: “So what? How can students actually USE this?” Provide: Concrete action steps Mindset shifts Tools or resources Questions for self-reflection Example: “To apply this knowledge: (1) Track your dopamine triggers using a simple journal—what activities leave you energized vs. drained? (2) Replace passive scrolling with ‘active consumption’ (e.g., annotating articles instead of skimming). (3) Experiment with 25-minute focus blocks (Pomodoro) to optimize reward cycles.”
CONCLUSION 75-100 words Restate your thesis in new words (don’t copy-paste) End with a memorable closing line: A thought-provoking question A call to action A bold prediction or statement Avoid: Generic endings like “In conclusion, sleep is important for students.”
CITATIONS (Minimum 2 Required) Format: Use APA 7 style Credibility Standards: ✓ Academic journals (PubMed, JSTOR, Google Scholar) ✓ Reputable news outlets (NYT, BBC, Scientific American) ✓ Primary sources (research papers, official reports, expert interviews) ✗ Wikipedia, personal blogs, unverified websites Citation Examples: Journal: Smith, J. (2023). Dopamine and motivation in adolescents. Journal of Neuroscience, 45(3), 120-135. News: Brown, A. (2024, January 15). The science of procrastination. The New York Times. https://nytimes.com/article Book: Clear, J. (2018). Atomic Habits. Penguin Random House. Include hyperlinks for all online sources.
FLEXIBILITY NOTE This template is a guide, not a straitjacket. If your article requires a different structure (e.g., an interview-based piece, a photo essay with commentary, a comparative analysis), that’s fine—but explain your reasoning in your submission notes.
SUBMISSION CHECKLIST (Before You Submit) Title is specific and engaging Thesis is clear and debatable At least 2 credible sources cited Word count is 500-1500 words Spellcheck and grammar check completed All hyperlinks work Real-world application section answers “So what?” Next Steps: Submit via the CogitoCommunity Google Form. You’ll receive feedback within 5-7 days. Revisions are normal — great writing is rewriting.
Goodluck.

content:
Submit an Article We’re always looking for original student perspectives. Here’s how to contribute:
Submission Guidelines Content Requirements Length: 500-1,500 words Sources: Minimum 2 credible sources (peer-reviewed journals, academic books, reputable publications) Originality: Must be your own work, not previously published by someone else Topic: Must fit Biology, Philosophy, Public Speaking themes or other clubs you want to suggest Format Use our article template Include citations in APA format Add 2-5 relevant tags Choose appropriate category and club for a detialed guide on how to write an article, see our Quick Reference Guide. insert google doc link or make it a page on the website
Content Policy Allowed: Global politics, theoretical discussions, evidence-based arguments Not Allowed: Local Lebanese politics, plagiarism, unsupported claims, personal attacks Review Process Submit via form below Review by editorial team (3-5 days) Feedback sent via email and/or whatsapp Revision if needed (2-3 days) Publication on website Average time from submission to publication: 7-10 days
Submit your form here.

Check our Idea Bank for 15 starter topics.
First time writing?
See our Quick Reference Guide for writing tips.
Questions?
Email us at: communitycogito@gmail.com (might change email soon) or dms on social media

About CogitoCommunity Our Philosophy Active Creation > Passive Consumption
CogitoCommunity is a bilingual student publication that rejects traditional “memorize and regurgitate” education. We believe in:
Applied Knowledge: Learning through creation, not consumption Student-Led Research: Ownership of intellectual pursuits Cross-Pollination: Biology meets Philosophy meets Public Speaking Critical Thinking: Question assumptions, defend positions, build arguments Our Mission To create a platform where students engage in meaningful intellectual discourse, produce original research, and develop skills that matter for college admissions, career readiness, and character development.
What We Cover Biology Club Anatomy, Cognitive Science, Pharmacology, Epidemiology — exploring how life works at every level.
Philosophy Club Logic, Ethics, Epistemology — examining fundamental questions and developing critical thinking frameworks.
Public Speaking Club Rhetoric, Debate, Persuasion — mastering the art of effective communication and argumentation.
The Team Co-Founders: Hussein Yassine & Maria Ghtaimi Core Contributors: to be announced
Content Policy We cover global and theoretical topics across all subjects. We do not publish content on local Lebanese politics to maintain focus on intellectual exploration rather than divisive local issues.
Contact Email: communitycogito@gmail.com (might change)

title: About CogitoCommunity

content:
WRITER INSTRUCTIONS
REQUIRED FIELDS
METADATA Title: [Engaging and specific—avoid vague titles like “The Importance of Sleep”]
Subtitle (Optional): [Clarify scope if needed]
Author: [Your Name]
Club Affiliation: [Biology / Philosophy / Public Speaking/ Politics (non-Lebanese)] Date Submitted: [MM/DD/YYYY] Word Count: [Auto-calculate or estimate] Category Tags: [e.g., Neuroscience, Ethics, Rhetoric]
SUMMARY BOX (Required – might skip for short articles) 75-100 words \ Write a standalone summary that answers: What is this article about? What will the reader learn? Why does it matter? Example: “This article explores how dopamine regulation affects student motivation and academic performance. By understanding the neuroscience of reward systems, students can design better study habits and avoid burnout. Practical takeaways include actionable strategies for managing screen time and optimizing focus.”
OPENING HOOK 100-150 words Start with one of the following: A provocative question (“Why do we procrastinate even when we know it hurts us?”) A surprising statistic (“70% of high school students report chronic sleep deprivation”) A vivid anecdote or scenario \ A challenge to conventional wisdom Purpose: Make the reader care. Establish why this topic matters to students right now. \
THESIS STATEMENT 1-2 sentences State your core argument or insight. It should be: Specific (not “Sleep is important” but “Polyphasic sleep schedules may harm long-term memory consolidation”) Debatable or Non-Obvious (if everyone already agrees, it’s not worth writing) \
BODY SECTIONS 300-1200 words (divided into 2-4 subsections) Use descriptive headers (e.g., “The Neuroscience of Habit Formation” instead of “Body Paragraph 1”). Each subsection should: Present Evidence: Cite research, data, expert opinions, or real-world examples Analyze, Don’t Summarize: Explain why this evidence matters and how it supports your thesis Connect to Your Argument: Every paragraph should ladder back to your main point Formatting Tips: Use bullet points or numbered lists sparingly (only when listing is clearer than prose) Include hyperlinks to sources within the text when referencing them Break up long paragraphs (aim for 3-5 sentences per paragraph) \
CRITICAL ANALYSIS (Optional for 500-700 words, Required for 1000+) 100-200 words This is NOT just “the other side of the argument.” Instead, consider: Limitations: What doesn’t this research account for? Counterarguments: What would a skeptic say? Nuance: Under what conditions might your thesis not hold true? Example: “While dopamine-driven gamification can boost short-term motivation, critics argue it may undermine intrinsic interest in learning. Students might become dependent on external rewards, reducing long-term intellectual curiosity. This limitation is worth considering when designing study systems.”
REAL-WORLD APPLICATION 100-150 words Answer: “So what? How can students actually USE this?” Provide: Concrete action steps Mindset shifts Tools or resources Questions for self-reflection Example: “To apply this knowledge: (1) Track your dopamine triggers using a simple journal—what activities leave you energized vs. drained? (2) Replace passive scrolling with ‘active consumption’ (e.g., annotating articles instead of skimming). (3) Experiment with 25-minute focus blocks (Pomodoro) to optimize reward cycles.”
CONCLUSION 75-100 words Restate your thesis in new words (don’t copy-paste) End with a memorable closing line: A thought-provoking question A call to action A bold prediction or statement Avoid: Generic endings like “In conclusion, sleep is important for students.”
CITATIONS (Minimum 2 Required) Format: Use APA 7 style Credibility Standards: ✓ Academic journals (PubMed, JSTOR, Google Scholar) ✓ Reputable news outlets (NYT, BBC, Scientific American) ✓ Primary sources (research papers, official reports, expert interviews) ✗ Wikipedia, personal blogs, unverified websites Citation Examples: Journal: Smith, J. (2023). Dopamine and motivation in adolescents. Journal of Neuroscience, 45(3), 120-135. News: Brown, A. (2024, January 15). The science of procrastination. The New York Times. https://nytimes.com/article Book: Clear, J. (2018). Atomic Habits. Penguin Random House. Include hyperlinks for all online sources.
FLEXIBILITY NOTE This template is a guide, not a straitjacket. If your article requires a different structure (e.g., an interview-based piece, a photo essay with commentary, a comparative analysis), that’s fine—but explain your reasoning in your submission notes.
SUBMISSION CHECKLIST (Before You Submit) Title is specific and engaging Thesis is clear and debatable At least 2 credible sources cited Word count is 500-1500 words Spellcheck and grammar check completed All hyperlinks work Real-world application section answers “So what?” Next Steps: Submit via the CogitoCommunity Google Form. You’ll receive feedback within 5-7 days. Revisions are normal — great writing is rewriting.
Goodluck.

title: COGITOCOMMUNITY ARTICLE TEMPLATE

content:
Submit an Article We’re always looking for original student perspectives. Here’s how to contribute:
Submission Guidelines Content Requirements Length: 500-1,500 words Sources: Minimum 2 credible sources (peer-reviewed journals, academic books, reputable publications) Originality: Must be your own work, not previously published by someone else Topic: Must fit Biology, Philosophy, Public Speaking themes or other clubs you want to suggest Format Use our article template Include citations in APA format Add 2-5 relevant tags Choose appropriate category and club for a detialed guide on how to write an article, see our Quick Reference Guide. insert google doc link or make it a page on the website
Content Policy Allowed: Global politics, theoretical discussions, evidence-based arguments Not Allowed: Local Lebanese politics, plagiarism, unsupported claims, personal attacks Review Process Submit via form below Review by editorial team (3-5 days) Feedback sent via email and/or whatsapp Revision if needed (2-3 days) Publication on website Average time from submission to publication: 7-10 days
Submit your form here.
Need Help? Not sure what to write about?
Check our Idea Bank for 15 starter topics.
First time writing?
See our Quick Reference Guide for writing tips.
Questions?
Email us at: communitycogito@gmail.com (might change email soon) or dms on social media

content:
WRITER INSTRUCTIONS
REQUIRED FIELDS
METADATA Title: [Engaging and specific—avoid vague titles like “The Importance of Sleep”]
Subtitle (Optional): [Clarify scope if needed]
Author: [Your Name]
Club Affiliation: [Biology / Philosophy / Public Speaking/ Politics (non-Lebanese)] Date Submitted: [MM/DD/YYYY] Word Count: [Auto-calculate or estimate] Category Tags: [e.g., Neuroscience, Ethics, Rhetoric]
SUMMARY BOX (Required – might skip for short articles) 75-100 words \ Write a standalone summary that answers: What is this article about? What will the reader learn? Why does it matter? Example: “This article explores how dopamine regulation affects student motivation and academic performance. By understanding the neuroscience of reward systems, students can design better study habits and avoid burnout. Practical takeaways include actionable strategies for managing screen time and optimizing focus.”
OPENING HOOK 100-150 words Start with one of the following: A provocative question (“Why do we procrastinate even when we know it hurts us?”) A surprising statistic (“70% of high school students report chronic sleep deprivation”) A vivid anecdote or scenario \ A challenge to conventional wisdom Purpose: Make the reader care. Establish why this topic matters to students right now. \
THESIS STATEMENT 1-2 sentences State your core argument or insight. It should be: Specific (not “Sleep is important” but “Polyphasic sleep schedules may harm long-term memory consolidation”) Debatable or Non-Obvious (if everyone already agrees, it’s not worth writing) \
BODY SECTIONS 300-1200 words (divided into 2-4 subsections) Use descriptive headers (e.g., “The Neuroscience of Habit Formation” instead of “Body Paragraph 1”). Each subsection should: Present Evidence: Cite research, data, expert opinions, or real-world examples Analyze, Don’t Summarize: Explain why this evidence matters and how it supports your thesis Connect to Your Argument: Every paragraph should ladder back to your main point Formatting Tips: Use bullet points or numbered lists sparingly (only when listing is clearer than prose) Include hyperlinks to sources within the text when referencing them Break up long paragraphs (aim for 3-5 sentences per paragraph) \
CRITICAL ANALYSIS (Optional for 500-700 words, Required for 1000+) 100-200 words This is NOT just “the other side of the argument.” Instead, consider: Limitations: What doesn’t this research account for? Counterarguments: What would a skeptic say? Nuance: Under what conditions might your thesis not hold true? Example: “While dopamine-driven gamification can boost short-term motivation, critics argue it may undermine intrinsic interest in learning. Students might become dependent on external rewards, reducing long-term intellectual curiosity. This limitation is worth considering when designing study systems.”
REAL-WORLD APPLICATION 100-150 words Answer: “So what? How can students actually USE this?” Provide: Concrete action steps Mindset shifts Tools or resources Questions for self-reflection Example: “To apply this knowledge: (1) Track your dopamine triggers using a simple journal—what activities leave you energized vs. drained? (2) Replace passive scrolling with ‘active consumption’ (e.g., annotating articles instead of skimming). (3) Experiment with 25-minute focus blocks (Pomodoro) to optimize reward cycles.”
CONCLUSION 75-100 words Restate your thesis in new words (don’t copy-paste) End with a memorable closing line: A thought-provoking question A call to action A bold prediction or statement Avoid: Generic endings like “In conclusion, sleep is important for students.”
CITATIONS (Minimum 2 Required) Format: Use APA 7 style Credibility Standards: ✓ Academic journals (PubMed, JSTOR, Google Scholar) ✓ Reputable news outlets (NYT, BBC, Scientific American) ✓ Primary sources (research papers, official reports, expert interviews) ✗ Wikipedia, personal blogs, unverified websites Citation Examples: Journal: Smith, J. (2023). Dopamine and motivation in adolescents. Journal of Neuroscience, 45(3), 120-135. News: Brown, A. (2024, January 15). The science of procrastination. The New York Times. https://nytimes.com/article Book: Clear, J. (2018). Atomic Habits. Penguin Random House. Include hyperlinks for all online sources.
FLEXIBILITY NOTE This template is a guide, not a straitjacket. If your article requires a different structure (e.g., an interview-based piece, a photo essay with commentary, a comparative analysis), that’s fine—but explain your reasoning in your submission notes.
SUBMISSION CHECKLIST (Before You Submit) Title is specific and engaging Thesis is clear and debatable At least 2 credible sources cited Word count is 500-1500 words Spellcheck and grammar check completed All hyperlinks work Real-world application section answers “So what?” Next Steps: Submit via the CogitoCommunity Google Form. You’ll receive feedback within 5-7 days. Revisions are normal — great writing is rewriting.
Goodluck.

content:
WRITER INSTRUCTIONS
REQUIRED FIELDS
METADATA Title: [Engaging and specific—avoid vague titles like “The Importance of Sleep”]
Subtitle (Optional): [Clarify scope if needed]
Author: [Your Name]
Club Affiliation: [Biology / Philosophy / Public Speaking/ Politics (non-Lebanese)] Date Submitted: [MM/DD/YYYY] Word Count: [Auto-calculate or estimate] Category Tags: [e.g., Neuroscience, Ethics, Rhetoric]
SUMMARY BOX (Required – might skip for short articles) 75-100 words \ Write a standalone summary that answers: What is this article about? What will the reader learn? Why does it matter? Example: “This article explores how dopamine regulation affects student motivation and academic performance. By understanding the neuroscience of reward systems, students can design better study habits and avoid burnout. Practical takeaways include actionable strategies for managing screen time and optimizing focus.”
OPENING HOOK 100-150 words Start with one of the following: A provocative question (“Why do we procrastinate even when we know it hurts us?”) A surprising statistic (“70% of high school students report chronic sleep deprivation”) A vivid anecdote or scenario \ A challenge to conventional wisdom Purpose: Make the reader care. Establish why this topic matters to students right now. \
THESIS STATEMENT 1-2 sentences State your core argument or insight. It should be: Specific (not “Sleep is important” but “Polyphasic sleep schedules may harm long-term memory consolidation”) Debatable or Non-Obvious (if everyone already agrees, it’s not worth writing) \
BODY SECTIONS 300-1200 words (divided into 2-4 subsections) Use descriptive headers (e.g., “The Neuroscience of Habit Formation” instead of “Body Paragraph 1”). Each subsection should: Present Evidence: Cite research, data, expert opinions, or real-world examples Analyze, Don’t Summarize: Explain why this evidence matters and how it supports your thesis Connect to Your Argument: Every paragraph should ladder back to your main point Formatting Tips: Use bullet points or numbered lists sparingly (only when listing is clearer than prose) Include hyperlinks to sources within the text when referencing them Break up long paragraphs (aim for 3-5 sentences per paragraph) \
CRITICAL ANALYSIS (Optional for 500-700 words, Required for 1000+) 100-200 words This is NOT just “the other side of the argument.” Instead, consider: Limitations: What doesn’t this research account for? Counterarguments: What would a skeptic say? Nuance: Under what conditions might your thesis not hold true? Example: “While dopamine-driven gamification can boost short-term motivation, critics argue it may undermine intrinsic interest in learning. Students might become dependent on external rewards, reducing long-term intellectual curiosity. This limitation is worth considering when designing study systems.”
REAL-WORLD APPLICATION 100-150 words Answer: “So what? How can students actually USE this?” Provide: Concrete action steps Mindset shifts Tools or resources Questions for self-reflection Example: “To apply this knowledge: (1) Track your dopamine triggers using a simple journal—what activities leave you energized vs. drained? (2) Replace passive scrolling with ‘active consumption’ (e.g., annotating articles instead of skimming). (3) Experiment with 25-minute focus blocks (Pomodoro) to optimize reward cycles.”
CONCLUSION 75-100 words Restate your thesis in new words (don’t copy-paste) End with a memorable closing line: A thought-provoking question A call to action A bold prediction or statement Avoid: Generic endings like “In conclusion, sleep is important for students.”
CITATIONS (Minimum 2 Required) Format: Use APA 7 style Credibility Standards: ✓ Academic journals (PubMed, JSTOR, Google Scholar) ✓ Reputable news outlets (NYT, BBC, Scientific American) ✓ Primary sources (research papers, official reports, expert interviews) ✗ Wikipedia, personal blogs, unverified websites Citation Examples: Journal: Smith, J. (2023). Dopamine and motivation in adolescents. Journal of Neuroscience, 45(3), 120-135. News: Brown, A. (2024, January 15). The science of procrastination. The New York Times. https://nytimes.com/article Book: Clear, J. (2018). Atomic Habits. Penguin Random House. Include hyperlinks for all online sources.
FLEXIBILITY NOTE This template is a guide, not a straitjacket. If your article requires a different structure (e.g., an interview-based piece, a photo essay with commentary, a comparative analysis), that’s fine—but explain your reasoning in your submission notes.
SUBMISSION CHECKLIST (Before You Submit) Title is specific and engaging Thesis is clear and debatable At least 2 credible sources cited Word count is 500-1500 words Spellcheck and grammar check completed All hyperlinks work Real-world application section answers “So what?” Next Steps: Submit via the CogitoCommunity Google Form. You’ll receive feedback within 5-7 days. Revisions are normal — great writing is rewriting.
Goodluck.

title: bio article test

content:
WRITER INSTRUCTIONS
REQUIRED FIELDS
METADATA Title: [Engaging and specific—avoid vague titles like “The Importance of Sleep”]
Subtitle (Optional): [Clarify scope if needed]
Author: [Your Name]
Club Affiliation: [Biology / Philosophy / Public Speaking/ Politics (non-Lebanese)] Date Submitted: [MM/DD/YYYY] Word Count: [Auto-calculate or estimate] Category Tags: [e.g., Neuroscience, Ethics, Rhetoric]
SUMMARY BOX (Required – might skip for short articles) 75-100 words \ Write a standalone summary that answers: What is this article about? What will the reader learn? Why does it matter? Example: “This article explores how dopamine regulation affects student motivation and academic performance. By understanding the neuroscience of reward systems, students can design better study habits and avoid burnout. Practical takeaways include actionable strategies for managing screen time and optimizing focus.”
OPENING HOOK 100-150 words Start with one of the following: A provocative question (“Why do we procrastinate even when we know it hurts us?”) A surprising statistic (“70% of high school students report chronic sleep deprivation”) A vivid anecdote or scenario \ A challenge to conventional wisdom Purpose: Make the reader care. Establish why this topic matters to students right now. \
THESIS STATEMENT 1-2 sentences State your core argument or insight. It should be: Specific (not “Sleep is important” but “Polyphasic sleep schedules may harm long-term memory consolidation”) Debatable or Non-Obvious (if everyone already agrees, it’s not worth writing) \
BODY SECTIONS 300-1200 words (divided into 2-4 subsections) Use descriptive headers (e.g., “The Neuroscience of Habit Formation” instead of “Body Paragraph 1”). Each subsection should: Present Evidence: Cite research, data, expert opinions, or real-world examples Analyze, Don’t Summarize: Explain why this evidence matters and how it supports your thesis Connect to Your Argument: Every paragraph should ladder back to your main point Formatting Tips: Use bullet points or numbered lists sparingly (only when listing is clearer than prose) Include hyperlinks to sources within the text when referencing them Break up long paragraphs (aim for 3-5 sentences per paragraph) \
CRITICAL ANALYSIS (Optional for 500-700 words, Required for 1000+) 100-200 words This is NOT just “the other side of the argument.” Instead, consider: Limitations: What doesn’t this research account for? Counterarguments: What would a skeptic say? Nuance: Under what conditions might your thesis not hold true? Example: “While dopamine-driven gamification can boost short-term motivation, critics argue it may undermine intrinsic interest in learning. Students might become dependent on external rewards, reducing long-term intellectual curiosity. This limitation is worth considering when designing study systems.”
REAL-WORLD APPLICATION 100-150 words Answer: “So what? How can students actually USE this?” Provide: Concrete action steps Mindset shifts Tools or resources Questions for self-reflection Example: “To apply this knowledge: (1) Track your dopamine triggers using a simple journal—what activities leave you energized vs. drained? (2) Replace passive scrolling with ‘active consumption’ (e.g., annotating articles instead of skimming). (3) Experiment with 25-minute focus blocks (Pomodoro) to optimize reward cycles.”
CONCLUSION 75-100 words Restate your thesis in new words (don’t copy-paste) End with a memorable closing line: A thought-provoking question A call to action A bold prediction or statement Avoid: Generic endings like “In conclusion, sleep is important for students.”
CITATIONS (Minimum 2 Required) Format: Use APA 7 style Credibility Standards: ✓ Academic journals (PubMed, JSTOR, Google Scholar) ✓ Reputable news outlets (NYT, BBC, Scientific American) ✓ Primary sources (research papers, official reports, expert interviews) ✗ Wikipedia, personal blogs, unverified websites Citation Examples: Journal: Smith, J. (2023). Dopamine and motivation in adolescents. Journal of Neuroscience, 45(3), 120-135. News: Brown, A. (2024, January 15). The science of procrastination. The New York Times. https://nytimes.com/article Book: Clear, J. (2018). Atomic Habits. Penguin Random House. Include hyperlinks for all online sources.
FLEXIBILITY NOTE This template is a guide, not a straitjacket. If your article requires a different structure (e.g., an interview-based piece, a photo essay with commentary, a comparative analysis), that’s fine—but explain your reasoning in your submission notes.
SUBMISSION CHECKLIST (Before You Submit) Title is specific and engaging Thesis is clear and debatable At least 2 credible sources cited Word count is 500-1500 words Spellcheck and grammar check completed All hyperlinks work Real-world application section answers “So what?” Next Steps: Submit via the CogitoCommunity Google Form. You’ll receive feedback within 5-7 days. Revisions are normal — great writing is rewriting.
Goodluck.

content:
About CogitoCommunity Our Philosophy Active Creation > Passive Consumption
CogitoCommunity is a bilingual student publication that rejects traditional “memorize and regurgitate” education. We believe in:
Applied Knowledge: Learning through creation, not consumption Student-Led Research: Ownership of intellectual pursuits Cross-Pollination: Biology meets Philosophy meets Public Speaking Critical Thinking: Question assumptions, defend positions, build arguments Our Mission To create a platform where students engage in meaningful intellectual discourse, produce original research, and develop skills that matter for college admissions, career readiness, and character development.
What We Cover Biology Club Anatomy, Cognitive Science, Pharmacology, Epidemiology — exploring how life works at every level.
Philosophy Club Logic, Ethics, Epistemology — examining fundamental questions and developing critical thinking frameworks.
Public Speaking Club Rhetoric, Debate, Persuasion — mastering the art of effective communication and argumentation.
The Team Co-Founders: Hussein Yassine & Maria Ghtaimi Core Contributors: to be announced
Content Policy We cover global and theoretical topics across all subjects. We do not publish content on local Lebanese politics to maintain focus on intellectual exploration rather than divisive local issues.
Contact Email: communitycogito@gmail.com (might change)

METADATA Title: [Engaging and specific—avoid vague titles like “The Importance of Sleep”]
Subtitle (Optional): [Clarify scope if needed]
Author: [Your Name]
Club Affiliation: [Biology / Philosophy / Public Speaking/ Politics (non-Lebanese)] Date Submitted: [MM/DD/YYYY] Word Count: [Auto-calculate or estimate] Category Tags: [e.g., Neuroscience, Ethics, Rhetoric]
SUMMARY BOX (Required – might skip for short articles) 75-100 words \ Write a standalone summary that answers: What is this article about? What will the reader learn? Why does it matter? Example: “This article explores how dopamine regulation affects student motivation and academic performance. By understanding the neuroscience of reward systems, students can design better study habits and avoid burnout. Practical takeaways include actionable strategies for managing screen time and optimizing focus.”
OPENING HOOK 100-150 words Start with one of the following: A provocative question (“Why do we procrastinate even when we know it hurts us?”) A surprising statistic (“70% of high school students report chronic sleep deprivation”) A vivid anecdote or scenario \ A challenge to conventional wisdom Purpose: Make the reader care. Establish why this topic matters to students right now. \
THESIS STATEMENT 1-2 sentences State your core argument or insight. It should be: Specific (not “Sleep is important” but “Polyphasic sleep schedules may harm long-term memory consolidation”) Debatable or Non-Obvious (if everyone already agrees, it’s not worth writing) \
BODY SECTIONS 300-1200 words (divided into 2-4 subsections) Use descriptive headers (e.g., “The Neuroscience of Habit Formation” instead of “Body Paragraph 1”). Each subsection should: Present Evidence: Cite research, data, expert opinions, or real-world examples Analyze, Don’t Summarize: Explain why this evidence matters and how it supports your thesis Connect to Your Argument: Every paragraph should ladder back to your main point Formatting Tips: Use bullet points or numbered lists sparingly (only when listing is clearer than prose) Include hyperlinks to sources within the text when referencing them Break up long paragraphs (aim for 3-5 sentences per paragraph) \
CRITICAL ANALYSIS (Optional for 500-700 words, Required for 1000+) 100-200 words This is NOT just “the other side of the argument.” Instead, consider: Limitations: What doesn’t this research account for? Counterarguments: What would a skeptic say? Nuance: Under what conditions might your thesis not hold true? Example: “While dopamine-driven gamification can boost short-term motivation, critics argue it may undermine intrinsic interest in learning. Students might become dependent on external rewards, reducing long-term intellectual curiosity. This limitation is worth considering when designing study systems.”
REAL-WORLD APPLICATION 100-150 words Answer: “So what? How can students actually USE this?” Provide: Concrete action steps Mindset shifts Tools or resources Questions for self-reflection Example: “To apply this knowledge: (1) Track your dopamine triggers using a simple journal—what activities leave you energized vs. drained? (2) Replace passive scrolling with ‘active consumption’ (e.g., annotating articles instead of skimming). (3) Experiment with 25-minute focus blocks (Pomodoro) to optimize reward cycles.”
CONCLUSION 75-100 words Restate your thesis in new words (don’t copy-paste) End with a memorable closing line: A thought-provoking question A call to action A bold prediction or statement Avoid: Generic endings like “In conclusion, sleep is important for students.”
CITATIONS (Minimum 2 Required) Format: Use APA 7 style Credibility Standards: ✓ Academic journals (PubMed, JSTOR, Google Scholar) ✓ Reputable news outlets (NYT, BBC, Scientific American) ✓ Primary sources (research papers, official reports, expert interviews) ✗ Wikipedia, personal blogs, unverified websites Citation Examples: Journal: Smith, J. (2023). Dopamine and motivation in adolescents. Journal of Neuroscience, 45(3), 120-135. News: Brown, A. (2024, January 15). The science of procrastination. The New York Times. https://nytimes.com/article Book: Clear, J. (2018). Atomic Habits. Penguin Random House. Include hyperlinks for all online sources.
FLEXIBILITY NOTE This template is a guide, not a straitjacket. If your article requires a different structure (e.g., an interview-based piece, a photo essay with commentary, a comparative analysis), that’s fine—but explain your reasoning in your submission notes.
SUBMISSION CHECKLIST (Before You Submit) Title is specific and engaging Thesis is clear and debatable At least 2 credible sources cited Word count is 500-1500 words Spellcheck and grammar check completed All hyperlinks work Real-world application section answers “So what?” Next Steps: Submit via the CogitoCommunity Google Form. You’ll receive feedback within 5-7 days. Revisions are normal — great writing is rewriting.
Goodluck.

About CogitoCommunity Our Philosophy Active Creation > Passive Consumption
CogitoCommunity is a bilingual student publication that rejects traditional “memorize and regurgitate” education. We believe in:
Applied Knowledge: Learning through creation, not consumption Student-Led Research: Ownership of intellectual pursuits Cross-Pollination: Biology meets Philosophy meets Public Speaking Critical Thinking: Question assumptions, defend positions, build arguments Our Mission To create a platform where students engage in meaningful intellectual discourse, produce original research, and develop skills that matter for college admissions, career readiness, and character development.
What We Cover Biology Club Anatomy, Cognitive Science, Pharmacology, Epidemiology — exploring how life works at every level.
Philosophy Club Logic, Ethics, Epistemology — examining fundamental questions and developing critical thinking frameworks.
Public Speaking Club Rhetoric, Debate, Persuasion — mastering the art of effective communication and argumentation.
The Team Co-Founders: Hussein Yassine & Maria Ghtaimi Core Contributors: to be announced
Content Policy We cover global and theoretical topics across all subjects. We do not publish content on local Lebanese politics to maintain focus on intellectual exploration rather than divisive local issues.

SUMMARY BOX (Required – might skip for short articles) 75-100 words \ Write a standalone summary that answers: What is this article about? What will the reader learn? Why does it matter? Example: “This article explores how dopamine regulation affects student motivation and academic performance. By understanding the neuroscience of reward systems, students can design better study habits and avoid burnout. Practical takeaways include actionable strategies for managing screen time and optimizing focus.”
OPENING HOOK 100-150 words Start with one of the following: A provocative question (“Why do we procrastinate even when we know it hurts us?”) A surprising statistic (“70% of high school students report chronic sleep deprivation”) A vivid anecdote or scenario \ A challenge to conventional wisdom Purpose: Make the reader care. Establish why this topic matters to students right now. \
THESIS STATEMENT 1-2 sentences State your core argument or insight. It should be: Specific (not “Sleep is important” but “Polyphasic sleep schedules may harm long-term memory consolidation”) Debatable or Non-Obvious (if everyone already agrees, it’s not worth writing) \
BODY SECTIONS 300-1200 words (divided into 2-4 subsections) Use descriptive headers (e.g., “The Neuroscience of Habit Formation” instead of “Body Paragraph 1”). Each subsection should: Present Evidence: Cite research, data, expert opinions, or real-world examples Analyze, Don’t Summarize: Explain why this evidence matters and how it supports your thesis Connect to Your Argument: Every paragraph should ladder back to your main point Formatting Tips: Use bullet points or numbered lists sparingly (only when listing is clearer than prose) Include hyperlinks to sources within the text when referencing them Break up long paragraphs (aim for 3-5 sentences per paragraph) \
CRITICAL ANALYSIS (Optional for 500-700 words, Required for 1000+) 100-200 words This is NOT just “the other side of the argument.” Instead, consider: Limitations: What doesn’t this research account for? Counterarguments: What would a skeptic say? Nuance: Under what conditions might your thesis not hold true? Example: “While dopamine-driven gamification can boost short-term motivation, critics argue it may undermine intrinsic interest in learning. Students might become dependent on external rewards, reducing long-term intellectual curiosity. This limitation is worth considering when designing study systems.”
REAL-WORLD APPLICATION 100-150 words Answer: “So what? How can students actually USE this?” Provide: Concrete action steps Mindset shifts Tools or resources Questions for self-reflection Example: “To apply this knowledge: (1) Track your dopamine triggers using a simple journal—what activities leave you energized vs. drained? (2) Replace passive scrolling with ‘active consumption’ (e.g., annotating articles instead of skimming). (3) Experiment with 25-minute focus blocks (Pomodoro) to optimize reward cycles.”
CONCLUSION 75-100 words Restate your thesis in new words (don’t copy-paste) End with a memorable closing line: A thought-provoking question A call to action A bold prediction or statement Avoid: Generic endings like “In conclusion, sleep is important for students.”
CITATIONS (Minimum 2 Required) Format: Use APA 7 style Credibility Standards: ✓ Academic journals (PubMed, JSTOR, Google Scholar) ✓ Reputable news outlets (NYT, BBC, Scientific American) ✓ Primary sources (research papers, official reports, expert interviews) ✗ Wikipedia, personal blogs, unverified websites Citation Examples: Journal: Smith, J. (2023). Dopamine and motivation in adolescents. Journal of Neuroscience, 45(3), 120-135. News: Brown, A. (2024, January 15). The science of procrastination. The New York Times. https://nytimes.com/article Book: Clear, J. (2018). Atomic Habits. Penguin Random House. Include hyperlinks for all online sources.
FLEXIBILITY NOTE This template is a guide, not a straitjacket. If your article requires a different structure (e.g., an interview-based piece, a photo essay with commentary, a comparative analysis), that’s fine—but explain your reasoning in your submission notes.
SUBMISSION CHECKLIST (Before You Submit) Title is specific and engaging Thesis is clear and debatable At least 2 credible sources cited Word count is 500-1500 words Spellcheck and grammar check completed All hyperlinks work Real-world application section answers “So what?” Next Steps: Submit via the CogitoCommunity Google Form. You’ll receive feedback within 5-7 days. Revisions are normal — great writing is rewriting.
Goodluck.

Applied Knowledge: Learning through creation, not consumption Student-Led Research: Ownership of intellectual pursuits Cross-Pollination: Biology meets Philosophy meets Public Speaking Critical Thinking: Question assumptions, defend positions, build arguments Our Mission To create a platform where students engage in meaningful intellectual discourse, produce original research, and develop skills that matter for college admissions, career readiness, and character development.

By understanding the neuroscience of reward systems, students can design better study habits and avoid burnout. Practical takeaways include actionable strategies for managing screen time and optimizing focus.”
OPENING HOOK 100-150 words Start with one of the following: A provocative question (“Why do we procrastinate even when we know it hurts us?”) A surprising statistic (“70% of high school students report chronic sleep deprivation”) A vivid anecdote or scenario \ A challenge to conventional wisdom Purpose: Make the reader care. Establish why this topic matters to students right now. \
THESIS STATEMENT 1-2 sentences State your core argument or insight. It should be: Specific (not “Sleep is important” but “Polyphasic sleep schedules may harm long-term memory consolidation”) Debatable or Non-Obvious (if everyone already agrees, it’s not worth writing) \
BODY SECTIONS 300-1200 words (divided into 2-4 subsections) Use descriptive headers (e.g., “The Neuroscience of Habit Formation” instead of “Body Paragraph 1”). Each subsection should: Present Evidence: Cite research, data, expert opinions, or real-world examples Analyze, Don’t Summarize: Explain why this evidence matters and how it supports your thesis Connect to Your Argument: Every paragraph should ladder back to your main point Formatting Tips: Use bullet points or numbered lists sparingly (only when listing is clearer than prose) Include hyperlinks to sources within the text when referencing them Break up long paragraphs (aim for 3-5 sentences per paragraph) \
CRITICAL ANALYSIS (Optional for 500-700 words, Required for 1000+) 100-200 words This is NOT just “the other side of the argument.” Instead, consider: Limitations: What doesn’t this research account for? Counterarguments: What would a skeptic say? Nuance: Under what conditions might your thesis not hold true? Example: “While dopamine-driven gamification can boost short-term motivation, critics argue it may undermine intrinsic interest in learning. Students might become dependent on external rewards, reducing long-term intellectual curiosity. This limitation is worth considering when designing study systems.”
REAL-WORLD APPLICATION 100-150 words Answer: “So what? How can students actually USE this?” Provide: Concrete action steps Mindset shifts Tools or resources Questions for self-reflection Example: “To apply this knowledge: (1) Track your dopamine triggers using a simple journal—what activities leave you energized vs. drained? (2) Replace passive scrolling with ‘active consumption’ (e.g., annotating articles instead of skimming). (3) Experiment with 25-minute focus blocks (Pomodoro) to optimize reward cycles.”
CONCLUSION 75-100 words Restate your thesis in new words (don’t copy-paste) End with a memorable closing line: A thought-provoking question A call to action A bold prediction or statement Avoid: Generic endings like “In conclusion, sleep is important for students.”
CITATIONS (Minimum 2 Required) Format: Use APA 7 style Credibility Standards: ✓ Academic journals (PubMed, JSTOR, Google Scholar) ✓ Reputable news outlets (NYT, BBC, Scientific American) ✓ Primary sources (research papers, official reports, expert interviews) ✗ Wikipedia, personal blogs, unverified websites Citation Examples: Journal: Smith, J. (2023). Dopamine and motivation in adolescents. Journal of Neuroscience, 45(3), 120-135. News: Brown, A. (2024, January 15). The science of procrastination. The New York Times. https://nytimes.com/article Book: Clear, J. (2018). Atomic Habits. Penguin Random House. Include hyperlinks for all online sources.
FLEXIBILITY NOTE This template is a guide, not a straitjacket. If your article requires a different structure (e.g., an interview-based piece, a photo essay with commentary, a comparative analysis), that’s fine—but explain your reasoning in your submission notes.
SUBMISSION CHECKLIST (Before You Submit) Title is specific and engaging Thesis is clear and debatable At least 2 credible sources cited Word count is 500-1500 words Spellcheck and grammar check completed All hyperlinks work Real-world application section answers “So what?” Next Steps: Submit via the CogitoCommunity Google Form. You’ll receive feedback within 5-7 days. Revisions are normal — great writing is rewriting.
Goodluck.
